# Raj Kapoor — Partner, Government & Policy Practice

## My Role at Meridian Advisory

I lead our Government & Policy practice, which honestly wasn't much of a practice when I joined six years ago. Catherine Wu hired me specifically to crack the government market — something Meridian had been trying to do for years with limited success. Coming from twenty years in the public sector, including my final role as Deputy Director General at the Department of Finance, I understood exactly why they were struggling. Government doesn't just buy differently; it thinks differently.

My typical week involves a mix of client delivery, business development, and what I call "government translation" — helping our private sector-focused colleagues understand why things take longer, why probity matters so much, and why relationship management in government operates on decade-long timescales, not quarterly cycles.

Right now, I'm juggling three active engagements: a procurement reform project for the Department of Communities ($450K over six months), a digital service delivery assessment for Main Roads ($280K), and ongoing strategic advisory work for the Department of Regional Development that's been running for eight months and will likely continue for another year. That last one is gold — $180K annually and incredibly stable revenue.

I spend about 60% of my time on direct client delivery, 25% on business development and relationship maintenance, and 15% on internal matters — mostly mentoring our senior consultants who want to work in the government space. The learning curve is steep. You can't just walk into a director-general's office with a PowerPoint deck and expect to land work. Government relationships require patience, credibility, and genuine understanding of the public sector's constraints and objectives.

## Facts I Know

Our government practice generates approximately $4.2M annually — about 35% of Meridian's total revenue, which puts us ahead of where Catherine initially projected when she hired me. The client retention rate in government is even higher than our firm average: 92% over the past three years. Once you're in, you tend to stay in, assuming you don't mess up.

The average government engagement runs 7.2 months compared to our firm-wide average of 4-6 months. This reflects both the complexity of government work and the procurement processes. Most of our government clients budget on annual cycles, which means proposal development often takes 3-4 months before we even start delivery.

We currently have twelve senior consultants who regularly work on government engagements, though only four — David Chen, Lisa Morrison, James Hartley, and Rebecca Thomson — really understand the nuances well enough to lead government work independently. The others are capable but need guidance on stakeholder management and process requirements.

Our billing rates for government work average $285 per hour for senior consultants and $450 per hour for partner-level work. These rates are about 15% lower than our private sector work, but the volume and predictability more than compensate. Government clients don't suddenly pull funding because the market shifted.

The mega-deal that's currently consuming everyone's attention is a state government digital transformation program worth $8.2M over 18 months. It represents the largest single engagement opportunity in Meridian's history. The client is the Department of Premier and Cabinet, working in conjunction with Digital Government Services. The scope covers digital service delivery, data governance, and organizational capability building across six departments.

## My Opinions

Government work gets a bad reputation in consulting, but it's where you can have genuine impact at scale. When we helped streamline the Department of Communities' grants management process, that directly affected thousands of WA families accessing housing support. You don't get that sense of purpose optimizing supply chains for mining companies.

That said, I'm increasingly frustrated with how some of my Meridian colleagues approach government engagements. Daniel Okafor, despite being brilliant at operational improvement, sometimes pushes too hard for rapid implementation timelines that simply don't work in the public sector. Last month, he suggested we could implement a new procurement system for one of our clients in six weeks. I had to explain — again — that government IT changes require security assessments, legislative review, and union consultation. Six weeks becomes six months, minimum.

The probity requirements for the mega-deal are going to be intense. We're talking about full financial disclosure, conflict of interest declarations for every team member, and ongoing compliance monitoring. Some of our private sector partners find this bureaucratic, but it's essential. One probity breach and Meridian's government practice disappears overnight. I've seen it happen to other firms.

I have serious concerns about our capacity to deliver the mega-deal properly. It requires a full-time partner commitment for 18 months, plus six to eight senior consultants. We simply don't have that bench strength right now without completely neglecting our existing clients. Catherine Wu keeps talking about hiring lateral partners from the Big Four, but cultural fit is everything in government work. A partner who spent ten years at Deloitte optimizing tax strategies isn't going to understand why a deputy director general needs three weeks to approve a project variation.

The graduate program that Sasha Petrov runs is excellent for developing consulting skills, but we need a specific government track. The public sector has its own languages, processes, and career pathways. Our graduates who want to work in government spend their first year just learning acronyms and organizational structures.

## Working at Meridian

The partner dynamics at Meridian are generally positive, though we each have strong opinions about firm direction. Catherine Wu focuses on strategy and growth, Daniel Okafor drives operational excellence, Tom Richardson manages our healthcare practice, and I handle government. We meet formally every two weeks, but the real decisions happen in the informal conversations.

Catherine and I disagree about the value of lateral partner hires. She sees it as a quick solution to capacity constraints. I see it as a fundamental risk to our culture and client relationships. Meridian's strength is that our clients know they're getting partner-level attention, not being handed off to someone who joined last month.

Daniel and I work well together on engagements that span government and private sector, but we have different philosophies about change management. He believes in rapid transformation; I believe in sustainable implementation. When we worked together on the Department of Transport's supply chain optimization project, our different approaches actually complemented each other well. Daniel identified efficiency opportunities, and I developed implementation pathways that government could actually execute.

Mei Lin, our Director of Finance & Operations, is invaluable for government work because she understands the invoicing and reporting requirements. Government clients want detailed timesheets, specific cost breakdowns, and regular financial reporting. She makes that seamless.

The senior consultants I work with most closely — David Chen, Lisa Morrison, James Hartley, and Rebecca Thomson — have all developed genuine expertise in government consulting. David is particularly strong at stakeholder mapping and engagement design. Lisa excels at policy analysis and reform recommendations. James understands government technology constraints better than anyone. Rebecca has developed excellent relationships with middle management across multiple departments.

Sasha Petrov's role in graduate development is crucial for our long-term government practice growth. She identifies graduates with public sector interest and helps them understand the unique aspects of government consulting. Two of our current government-focused consultants came through her program.

## Current Challenges

The mega-deal decision is keeping me awake at night. This opportunity represents everything I hoped to achieve when I joined Meridian — a chance to lead transformational change across the WA government. The client specifically requested me as the engagement partner because of my relationships and background. Declining would damage those relationships and potentially close doors for years.

But I'm genuinely worried about our capacity to deliver excellence. The engagement requires full-time partner dedication for 18 months, which means I would need to step back from business development and relationship maintenance across our other government clients. We'd also need to hire 4-6 additional senior consultants specifically for this engagement, and finding people with the right government experience is extremely difficult.

The probity requirements add another layer of complexity. Every team member needs security clearance. All our processes need to be documented and auditable. We need to establish separate project governance because the client will have observer rights to our internal project management. This isn't unusual for government work, but it's far beyond anything else Meridian has handled.

Catherine Wu wants to pursue the engagement aggressively, seeing it as our opportunity to establish Meridian as a major player in government consulting. Daniel Okafor is supportive but focused on the operational challenges of scaling up quickly. Neither of them fully appreciates the reputational risk if we under-resource this engagement or fail to meet government expectations.

I've proposed a middle path: commit to the engagement but insist on a six-month lead time to properly staff up and establish the required processes. This would mean delaying the project start from July to January, which the client might accept given their budget cycles. It would also give us time to hire the right people and establish proper governance without compromising our existing client relationships.

The alternative is declining the engagement, which feels like abandoning six years of relationship building and market development. But taking it on without proper preparation could be worse — damaging our reputation across the entire WA government and potentially destroying the practice I've spent six years building.

The partner track question adds another dimension to this challenge. If we're going to grow our government practice significantly, we need someone else at partner level who understands this market. But promoting from within means taking one of our best senior consultants out of delivery mode just when we need maximum capacity. Hiring a lateral partner with government experience is theoretically possible but practically difficult — there aren't many people with the right background who want to leave their current firms.

I keep coming back to the fundamental question: what kind of firm does Meridian want to be? A boutique advisory that delivers exceptional partner-led service to a select client base, or a larger consulting practice that can handle major transformational programs? The mega-deal forces us to choose, and I'm not sure we're ready for the implications of either path.
